title: Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management (FRBM) Act
focus: Provisions, targets, and recent CAG compliance findings regarding the FRBM Act.
rationale: The FRBM Act is a core macroeconomic topic for UPSC, with specific targets and recent CAG findings providing excellent material for statement-based questions.
sectionExcerpt: The FRBM Act, 2003 aims to foster inter-generational equity and macroeconomic stability. It mandates the CAG to conduct an annual compliance review and requires three statements to be laid before Parliament: Macro-economic Framework, Medium Term Fiscal Policy, and Fiscal Policy Strategy. Key targets include a fiscal deficit of 3% of GDP, general government debt limit of 60% (Central 40%), and additional guarantees capped at 0.5% of GDP. The 2018 amendment removed Revenue and Effective Revenue Deficit targets. The recent CAG report noted central government debt peaked at 61.38% in FY21, while unrealized tax revenues surged to 21.30 trillion by FY23.
evidenceBullets: FRBM Act mandates CAG to conduct an annual compliance review.
Requires Macro-economic Framework, Medium Term Fiscal Policy, and Fiscal Policy Strategy statements with the Budget.
Original fiscal deficit target is 3% of GDP; revised path targets under 4.5%.
Debt limits set at 60% for General Government and 40% for Central Government.
Targets for Revenue and Effective Revenue Deficit were removed by the 2018 Amendment.
CAG reported central government debt-to-GDP peaked at 61.38% in FY 2020-21.

title: Key Fiscal Terms and Deficits
focus: Definitions and implications of various government deficits and fiscal slippage.
rationale: Conceptual clarity on deficits is frequently tested in UPSC Prelims, especially the distinctions between fiscal, primary, revenue, and effective revenue deficits.
sectionExcerpt: Fiscal Deficit is the difference between total expenditure and total receipts excluding borrowing. Primary Deficit is Fiscal Deficit minus interest payments. Revenue Deficit is the excess of revenue expenditure over revenue receipts, representing net dissaving. Effective Revenue Deficit is Revenue Deficit minus grants for the creation of capital assets. Fiscal Slippage occurs when actual fiscal performance deviates from targets, often due to farm loan waivers or tax cuts.
evidenceBullets: Fiscal Deficit excludes borrowing from total receipts.
Primary Deficit is calculated by subtracting interest payments from Fiscal Deficit.
Revenue Deficit represents net dissaving for present consumption without capital formation.
Effective Revenue Deficit excludes grants for creation of Capital Assets from Revenue Deficit.
Fiscal Slippage can be caused by farm loan waivers or reductions in tax collections like GST and VAT.

title: Indian Tax Structure: Direct vs. Indirect Taxes
focus: Characteristics, administration, and examples of direct and indirect taxes in India.
rationale: The distinction between progressive direct taxes and regressive indirect taxes, along with their respective administrative bodies, forms the foundational basis of taxation questions.
sectionExcerpt: The Indian tax structure is divided into Direct and Indirect taxes. Direct taxes (e.g., Income, Corporate, Capital Gains) are progressive, administered by the CBDT, and their incidence and impact fall on the same person. Indirect taxes (e.g., GST, Customs, Excise) are regressive, administered by the CBIC, and their burden is shifted to the consumer. Direct taxes are harder to collect due to evasion risks, whereas indirect taxes are embedded in prices.
evidenceBullets: Direct taxes are progressive in nature, meaning higher income attracts a higher tax rate.
The burden of direct taxes cannot be shifted, whereas the burden of indirect taxes is shifted to the buyer.
Direct taxes are administered by the CBDT, while indirect taxes are administered by the CBIC.
Indirect taxes are regressive, applying the same rate regardless of income.
Excise duty on petroleum and liquor remains outside the GST framework.
